body.label: func_metric_11.root.thm
body.claim: Let $X = \mathbb{R}$ and define $d: \mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ by $d(x,y)=|x^3-y^3|$. Then $d$ is a metric on $\mathbb{R}$.

proof_steps.statement: Define d(x,y) = |x^3 - y^3| for all x,y ∈ R.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: A function d on a set X is a metric if it satisfies non-negativity, identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, and the triangle inequality for all points of X.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Metric axioms
deduced_from_theorem.description: Used to reduce the goal to verifying the four metric properties.

proof_steps.claim: For all x,y,z ∈ R, d(x,y) ≥ 0, d(x,y)=0 if and only if x=y, d(x,y)=d(y,x), and d(x,z) ≤ d(x,y)+d(y,z).
proof_steps.proof_method: By the definition of a metric.
By non-negativity of absolute value applied to t = x^3 - y^3.
Substitute the definition of d into the preceding inequality.

proof_steps.statement: Fix arbitrary x,y ∈ R for identity of indiscernibles.

proof_steps.proof_method: By the definition of d and the assumption d(x,y)=0.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For every real number t, |t| = 0 if and only if t = 0.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Zero absolute value criterion

deduced_from_theorem.claim: The function f:R→R defined by f(t)=t^3 is strictly increasing and hence injective on R.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Injectivity of the cubic function
deduced_from_theorem.description: Used to infer x=y from x^3=y^3.

proof_steps.proof_method: Since t ↦ t^3 is injective on R.
Cubing equal real numbers preserves equality.

proof_steps.claim: d(x,y) = 0 if and only if x = y.

proof_steps.statement: Fix arbitrary x,y ∈ R for symmetry.
Fix arbitrary x,y,z ∈ R for the triangle inequality.

proof_steps.proof_method: This is the triangle inequality axiom for the fixed points x,y,z.

proof_steps.claim: d(x,z) ≤ d(x,y) + d(y,z) if and only if |x^3 - z^3| ≤ |x^3 - y^3| + |y^3 - z^3|.
proof_steps.proof_method: Rewrite each occurrence of d by its definition.
Substitute the definitions of a and b and simplify.

deduced_from_theorem.name: Triangle inequality for absolute value
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to a = x^3-y^3 and b = y^3-z^3.

proof_steps.proof_method: Apply the standard triangle inequality for absolute value and use a+b=x^3-z^3.
Rewrite the inequality by the definition of d.

deduced_from_claim: For all x,y ∈ R, d(x,y)=0 iff x=y.
For all x,y,z ∈ R, d(x,z) ≤ d(x,y)+d(y,z).

deduced_from_theorem.claim: A function d on a set X is a metric if it satisfies non-negativity, identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, and the triangle inequality for all points of X.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Metric axioms
deduced_from_theorem.description: Used to conclude that d is a metric after all four axioms are verified.

proof_steps.claim: d is a metric on R.
proof_steps.proof_method: All four metric axioms have been verified for d(x,y)=|x^3-y^3|.

proof.text:
Let $X = \mathbb{R}$ and define $d : \mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ by $d(x,y) = |x^{3} - y^{3}|$ for all $x,y \in \mathbb{R}$. The goal is to show that $d$ is a metric on $\mathbb{R}$. This means that we must verify the following four properties for all $x,y,z \in \mathbb{R}$:

1. $d(x,y) \ge 0$ (non-negativity),
2. $d(x,y) = 0$ if and only if $x = y$ (identity of indiscernibles),
3. $d(x,y) = d(y,x)$ (symmetry),
4. $d(x,z) \le d(x,y) + d(y,z)$ (triangle inequality).

Each property will be checked in turn.

1. For non-negativity, fix arbitrary $x,y \in \mathbb{R}$. By definition, $d(x,y) = |x^{3} - y^{3}|$. The absolute value $|t|$ of any real number $t$ is always greater than or equal to $0$. Applying this to $t = x^{3} - y^{3}$, we obtain $|x^{3} - y^{3}| \ge 0$. Therefore $d(x,y) \ge 0$ for all $x,y \in \mathbb{R}$.

2. For the identity of indiscernibles, fix arbitrary $x,y \in \mathbb{R}$. First assume that $d(x,y) = 0$. Then $|x^{3} - y^{3}| = 0$. For any real number $t$, we have $|t| = 0$ if and only if $t = 0$. Applying this to $t = x^{3} - y^{3}$, it follows that $x^{3} - y^{3} = 0$, hence $x^{3} = y^{3}$. The function $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ defined by $f(t) = t^{3}$ is strictly increasing and hence injective on $\mathbb{R}$. In particular, if $x^{3} = y^{3}$ then $x = y$. Thus from $d(x,y) = 0$ we conclude $x = y$.

Conversely, assume $x = y$. Then $x^{3} = y^{3}$, so $x^{3} - y^{3} = 0$. Taking absolute values, we obtain $|x^{3} - y^{3}| = |0| = 0$, hence $d(x,y) = 0$. Combining the two directions, we have shown that $d(x,y) = 0$ if and only if $x = y$.

3. For symmetry, fix arbitrary $x,y \in \mathbb{R}$. By definition,
\[
d(x,y) = |x^{3} - y^{3}|.
\]
On the other hand,
\[
d(y,x) = |y^{3} - x^{3}|.
\]
Since $y^{3} - x^{3} = -(x^{3} - y^{3})$, we have
\[
d(y,x) = |y^{3} - x^{3}| = |-(x^{3} - y^{3})|.
\]
For any real number $t$, we have $|-t| = |t|$. Applying this to $t = x^{3} - y^{3}$, we obtain $|-(x^{3} - y^{3})| = |x^{3} - y^{3}|$. Therefore
\[
d(y,x) = |y^{3} - x^{3}| = |x^{3} - y^{3}| = d(x,y).
\]
This proves symmetry.

4. For the triangle inequality, fix arbitrary $x,y,z \in \mathbb{R}$. We must show that
\[
d(x,z) \le d(x,y) + d(y,z).
\]
By definition of $d$, this inequality becomes
\[
|x^{3} - z^{3}| \le |x^{3} - y^{3}| + |y^{3} - z^{3}|.
\]
We use the standard triangle inequality for the absolute value on $\mathbb{R}$, which states that for all real numbers $a,b$ we have
\[
|a + b| \le |a| + |b|.
\]
Apply this with
\[
a = x^{3} - y^{3} \quad \text{and} \quad b = y^{3} - z^{3}.
\]
Then $a + b = (x^{3} - y^{3}) + (y^{3} - z^{3}) = x^{3} - z^{3}$. Therefore
\[
|x^{3} - z^{3}| = |(x^{3} - y^{3}) + (y^{3} - z^{3})| \le |x^{3} - y^{3}| + |y^{3} - z^{3}|.
\]
By the definition of $d$, this inequality can be rewritten as
\[
d(x,z) \le d(x,y) + d(y,z).
\]
This establishes the triangle inequality.

Since all four metric axioms have been verified for the function $d(x,y) = |x^{3} - y^{3}|$ on $\mathbb{R}$, the function $d$ is a metric on $\mathbb{R}$.
